greek_head: Τί γὰρ τὸ φιλοκερδές τί ποτέ ἐστιν καὶ τίνες οἱ φιλοκερδεῖς
greek_tokens: tis gar o filokerdhs tis pote eimi kai tinw e filokerdew egw men dokew e an kerdainw aciaw apo o mhdeis acios poteros oun su dokew gignwskw ostis oudeis eimi acios h agnoew ei gar agnoew anohtos legw o filokerdew alla ou anohtos legos alla panourgos kai
english_head: And what is love of gain? What can it be, and who are the lovers of gain? In my opinion, they are those who think it worth while to make gain out of things of no worth. Is it your opinion that they know those things to be of no worth, or do not know? For if they do not know, you mean that the lovers of gain are fools. No, I do not mean they are fools, but rascals who wickedly yield to gain, because they know that the things out of which they dare to make their gain are worthless,

greek_head: πονηροὺς καὶ ἥττους τοῦ κέρδους γιγνώσκοντας ὅτι οὐδενὸς ἄξιά ἐστιν ἀφ' ὧν τολμῶσι κερδαίνειν ὅμως τολμᾶν φιλοκερδεῖν
greek_tokens: ponhros kai hsswn o kerdos gignwskw ostis oudeis acios eimi apo os tolmaw kerdainw omws tolma filokerdew dia anaisxuntia ara oun toiosde legw o filokerdhs oios ean futeuw gewrgos anhr kai gignwskw ostis oudeis acios o futon aciow apo outos ektrefw kerdainw ara toioutos autos legw apo pas o ge filokerdhs eimi swkraths oiomai dew kerdainw mh egw outws eikazw wsper tis adikew upo tis
english_head: and yet they dare to be lovers of gain from mere shamelessness. Well now, do you mean by the lover of gain such a man, for instance, as a farmer who plants something which he knows is a worthless herb, and thinks fit to make gain out of it when he has reared it up? Is that the sort of man you mean? The lover of gain, as such, Socrates, thinks he ought to make gain from everything. Please do not speak so recklessly, as though you had been wronged by someone,

greek_head: ἀλλὰ προσέχων ἐμοὶ τὸν νοῦν ἀπόκριναι ὥσπερ ἂν εἰ ἐξ ἀρχῆς πάλιν ἠρώτων οὐχὶ ὁμολογεῖς τὸν φιλοκερδῆ ἐπιστήμονα
greek_tokens: alla prosexw egw o new apokrinw wsper an ei ek arxh palin erwtaw ou omologew o filokerdhs episthmwn eimi peri o acios outos oqen kerdainw aciow egw tis oun episthmos peri futon o acios en opoios acios futeuw kai wra kai xwra ina tis kai egw o sofos rhma emballw os e decioomai peri o dikh kalliepeomai
english_head: but give me your attention and answer just as you would if I were beginning my questions over again. Do you not admit that the lover of gain has knowledge of the worth of the thing from which he thinks it worth while to make gain? I do. Then who has knowledge of the worth of plants, and of the sort of season and soil in which they are worth planting—if we too may throw in one of those artful phrases which adroit pleaders use to trick out their speeches in the law courts?

greek_head: Ἐγὼ μὲν οἶμαι γεωργόν Τὸ οὖν ἀξιοῦν κερδαίνειν ἄλλο τι λέγεις ἢ οἴεσθαι
greek_tokens: egw men oimh gewrgos o oun aciow kerdainw allos tis legw h oiomai dew kerdainw outos legos mh toinun egw epixeirew ecapath anhr presbus
english_head: For my part, I should say a farmer. And by “think it worth while to make gain” do you mean aught but “thinking one ought to make gain”? I mean that. Then do not attempt to deceive me, who am now quite an elderly person,

greek_head: ἤδη οὕτω νέος ὤν ἀποκρινόμενος ὥσπερ νυνδὴ ἃ οὐδ' αὐτὸς οἴει ἀλλ' ὡς ἀληθῶς εἰπέ ἆρ' ἔστιν ὅντινα οἴει
greek_tokens: hdh outws neos eimi apokrinw wsper nundi o oude autos oiomai alla ws alhqhs eipon ara eimi ostis oiomai gewrgikos anhr gignomai kai gignwskw ostis oudeis acios futeuw o futon oiomai apo outos kerdainw ma diw ou egw tis de ippikos anhr gignwskw ostis oudeis acios sition o ippos paraxew agnoew autos oiomai ostis o ippos diafqeirw ou egw
english_head: and you so young, by making, as you did just now, an answer that is not even your own thought; but tell me in all truth, do you suppose that any man who was taking up farming and who knew it was a worthless plant that he was planting, could think to make gain from it? Upon my word, I do not. Or again, take a horseman who knows that he is providing worthless food for his horse; do you suppose he is unaware that he is destroying his horse? I do not.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἄρα οἴεταί γε ἀπὸ τούτων κερδαίνειν τῶν σιτίων τῶν μηδενὸς ἀξίων
greek_tokens: ou ara oiomai ge apo outos kerdainw o siteomai o mhdeis acios ou tis de kubernaw mhdeis acios estia kai phdalion o naias paraskeuazw agnoew oiomai ostis zhmiow kai kinduneuw kai autos apollumi kai o naus apollumi kai o an agh pas ou egw ou ara oiomai ge kerdainw apo o skeuos o
english_head: So he does not think to make gain from that worthless food. No. Or again, take a navigator who has furnished his ship with worthless spars and ropes; do you think he is unaware that he will suffer for it, and will be in danger of being lost himself, and of losing the ship and all her cargo? I do not. So he does not think to make gain from

greek_head: μηδενὸς ἀξίων Οὐ γάρ
greek_tokens: mhdeis acios ou gar alla strathgos gignwskw ostis o stratia autos oudeis acios oplon exis oiomai apo outos kerdainw kai aciow kerdainw oudamos alla aulhths aulos oudeis acios exw h kiqarisths lura h tocoths tocon h allos ostis sullhbdhn o dhmiourgew h o allos o emfrwn androw mhdeis acios organon h allos paraskeuh hntinaoun exw apo outos oiomai kerdainw
english_head: that worthless tackle? No, indeed. But does a general, who knows that his army has worthless arms, think to make gain, or think it worth while to make gain, from them? By no means. Or does a flute-player who has worthless flutes, or a harper with a lyre, a bowman with a bow, or anyone else at all, in short, among ordinary craftsmen or sensible men in general, with any implement or other equipment of any sort that is worthless, think to make gain from it?

greek_head: Οὔκουν φαίνεταί γε Τίνας οὖν ποτε λέγεις τοὺς φιλοκερδεῖς οὐ γάρ που
greek_tokens: oukoun fainw ge tis oun pote legw o filokerdew ou gar pou outos ge os dierxomai alla ostis gignwskw o oudeis acios apo outos oiomai dew kerdainw alla outws men eimi qaumasios ws su legw ou este anqrwpos oudeis filokerdhs alla egw eimi swkraths boulomai legw outos filokerdew eimi e ekastote upo aplhstia kai panu
english_head: To all appearance, no. Then whoever can they be, your lovers of gain? For I presume they are not the people whom we have successively mentioned, but people who know their worthless things, and yet think they are to make gain from them. But in that case, by what you say, remarkable sir, no man alive is a lover of gain Well, Socrates, I should like to call those lovers of gain who from insatiable greed consumedly long for things that are even quite petty and of little or no worth,

greek_head: σμικρὰ καὶ ὀλίγου ἄξια καὶ οὐδενὸς γλίχονται ὑπερφυῶς καὶ φιλοκερδοῦσιν
greek_tokens: mikros kai oligos acios kai oudeis glixomai uperfuhs kai filokerdew ou dhpou eimi beltistos gignwskw ostis oudeis acios eimi outos men gar hdh egw autos o logos ecelegxw ostis adunatos egw dokew oukoun ei mh gignwskw dhlos ostis agnoew oiomai de o oudeis acios polus acios eimi fainw allos tis oun e ge filokerdew filew o kerdos nai kerdos de legw enantion o zhmia
english_head: and so love gain, in each case. Not knowing, of course, my excellent friend, that the things are worthless; for we have already convinced ourselves by our argument that this is impossible. I agree. And if not knowing this, clearly they are ignorant of it, but think that those worthless things are worth a great deal. Apparently. Now, of course lovers of gain must love gain? Yes. And by gain you mean the opposite of loss?

greek_head: Ἔγωγε Ἔστιν οὖν ὅτῳ ἀγαθόν ἐστι ζημιοῦσθαι
greek_tokens: egw eimi oun ostis agaqos eimi zhmiow oudeis alla kakos nai blaptw upo o zhmia ara anqrwpos blaptw kakos ara o zhmia nai enantion de o zhmia o kerdos enantion agaqos ara o kerdos nai
english_head: I do. And is it a good thing for anyone to suffer loss? For no one. Rather an evil? Yes. So mankind are harmed by loss. They are harmed. Then loss is an evil. Yes. And gain is the opposite of loss. The opposite. So that gain is a good. Yes. Hence it is those who love the good that you call lovers of gain. So it seems.

greek_head: Τοὺς οὖν τὸ ἀγαθὸν φιλοῦντας φιλοκερδεῖς καλεῖς Ἔοικεν
greek_tokens: o oun o agaqos filew filokerdew kalew eoika ou manikos ge eimi etairos legw o filokerdew alla su autos poteros filew o an agaqos eimi h ou filew egw eimi de tis agaqos o ou filew alla kakos ma diw ou egw alla pas o agaqos isos filew nai eromai dh kai egw ei ou kai egw omologew gar kai
english_head: At least there is nothing mad, my friend, about lovers of gain, as you describe them. But tell me, do you yourself love, or not love, whatever is good? I love it. And is there anything good that you do not love, or must it then be evil? Upon my word, nothing. In fact, I expect you love all good things. Yes. Well now, ask me on my side whether I do not likewise: for I shall agree with you, for my part, that I love good things. But besides you and me, do you not think that all the rest of mankind

greek_head: ἐγώ σοι φιλεῖν τἀγαθά ἀλλὰ πρὸς ἐμοὶ καὶ σοὶ οἱ ἄλλοι ἄνθρωποι ἅπαντες οὐ δοκοῦσί σοι τἀγαθὰ φιλεῖν τὰ δὲ κακὰ
greek_tokens: egw su filew agaqos alla pros egw kai sos e allos anqrwpos apas ou dokew su agaqos filew o de kakos misew egw fainw o de kerdos agaqos omologew nai pas au filokerdew fainw outos o tropos os de o proteros legw oudeis eimi filokerdhs poteros oun an tis o logos xraomai ou an ecamartanw ei tis eimi swkraths oimh orqos lambanw o
english_head: love good things, and hate evil things? It appears so to me. And we admitted that gain is good? Yes. On this new showing, everyone appears to be a lover of gain; whereas, by our former way of arguing, no one was a lover of gain. So on which of the two arguments are we to rely, in order to avoid error? What has to be done, I think, Socrates, is to conceive the lover of gain rightly. The right view of the lover of gain is that he is one who concerns himself with,

greek_head: φιλοκερδῆ ὀρθῶς δ' ἐστὶ τοῦτον ἡγεῖσθαι φιλοκερδῆ ὃς ἂν σπουδάζῃ ἐπὶ τούτοις καὶ ἀξιοῖ κερδαίνειν ἀπ' αὐτῶν ἀφ' ὧν
greek_tokens: filokerdhs orqos de eimi outos hgeomai filokerdhs os an spoudazw epi outos kai aciow kerdainw apo autos apo os e xrhstos ou tolmaw kerdainw alla oraw eimi glukus o kerdainw arti omologew eimi wfelew tis oun dh outos ostis kai ode autos prosomologew boulomai o agaqos pas kai aei nai oukoun kai e agaqos pas o kerdos boulomai exw eiper agaqos ge eimi
english_head: and thinks fit to make gain from, things from which honest men do not dare to make gain. But you see, my sweet sir, we have just admitted that making gain is being benefited. Well, what of that? There is the further point we have admitted in addition to this—that all men wish for good things always. Yes. Then good men likewise wish to have all gains, if these are good things.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἀφ' ὧν γε μέλλουσιν ὦ Σώκρατες βλαβήσεσθαι τῶν κερδῶν
greek_tokens: ou apo os ge mellw eimi swkraths blaptw o kerdos blaptw de legw zhmiow h allos tis ou alla zhmiow legos upo o kerdos oun zhmiow h upo o zhmia anqrwpos upo amfoteros kai gar upo o zhmia zhmiow kai upo o kerdos o ponhros eimi dokew oun tis su xrhstos kai agaqos pragma ponhros eimi ou egw
english_head: Not those gains from which they are bound, Socrates, to suffer harm. By “suffer harm” do you mean “suffer loss,” or something else? No, I mean just “suffer loss.” Well, do men suffer loss from gain or from loss? From both; for they suffer loss from loss and from wicked gain. Pray now, do you consider that any useful and good thing is wicked? I do not.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ὡμολογήσαμεν ὀλίγον πρότερον τὸ κέρδος τῇ ζημίᾳ κακῷ ὄντι ἐναντίον εἶναι
greek_tokens: oukoun omologew oligos proteros o kerdos o zhmia kakazw eimi enantion eimi fhmi enantion de eimi kakazw agaqos eimi omologew gar oraw oun epixeirew egw ecapath epithdes enantios legos os arti omologew ou ma dios eimi swkraths alla enantion su egw ecapataw kai ou oida oph en o logos anoos kai katos strefw
english_head: And we admitted a little while ago that gain is the opposite of loss, which is an evil. I agree. And that, being the opposite of an evil, it is good? That was our admission. So you see, you are attempting to deceive me, for you deliberately contradict what we agreed to just now. No, on my honor, Socrates; on the contrary, it is you who are deceiving me, by twisting this way and that so perplexingly in your talk.

greek_head: Εὐφήμει οὐ μεντἂν καλῶς ποιοίην οὐ πειθόμενος ἀνδρὶ ἀγαθῷ καὶ σοφῷ
greek_tokens: eufhmew ou mentoi kalos poiew ou peiqw andris agaqos kai sofos tis outos kai tis malista poliths men emos te kai sos peisistratos de uios o ek filaidhs ipparxos os o peisistratos pais eimi presbus kai sofos os allos te polus kai kalos ergon sofia apodeiknumi kai o omhros epos proteros komizw eis o gaia outos kai anagkazw o raywdos panaqhnaia ek upolhyis efechs autos dieimi wsper nun eti
english_head: Hush, hush! Why, surely it would be wrong of me not to obey a good and wise person. Who is that? And to what are you referring now? I mean my and your fellow-citizen, Pisistratus’s son Hipparchus, of Philaidae, who was the eldest and wisest of Pisistratus’s sons, and who, among the many goodly proofs of wisdom that he showed, first brought the poems of Homer into this country of ours, and compelled the rhapsodes at the Panathenaea to recite them in relay, one man following on another, as

greek_head: οἵδε ποιοῦσιν καὶ ἐπ' Ἀνακρέοντα τὸν Τήιον πεντηκόντορον στείλας ἐκόμισεν εἰς τὴν πόλιν Σιμωνίδην δὲ τὸν Κεῖον
greek_tokens: ode poiew kai epi anakrewn o thios penthkontoros stellw komizw eis o polis simwnidhs de o kaiw aei peri eautou exw megas misqos kai dwron peiqw outos de poiew boulomai paideuw o poliths ina ws beltistos eimi autos arxw ou oiomai dew oudeis sofia fqonew ate eimi kalos te agaqos epei de autos e peri o astu o poliths paideuw eimi kai
english_head: they still do now. He dispatched a fifty-oared galley for Anacreon of Teos, and brought him into our city. Simonides of Ceos he always had about him, prevailing on him by plenteous fees and gifts. All this he did from a wish to educate the citizens, in order that he might have subjects of the highest excellence; for he thought it not right to grudge wisdom to any, so noble and good was he. And when his people in the city had been educated and were admiring him for his wisdom,

greek_head: ἐθαύμαζον αὐτὸν ἐπὶ σοφίᾳ ἐπιβουλεύων αὖ τοὺς ἐν τοῖς ἀγροῖς παιδεῦσαι ἔστησεν αὐτοῖς Ἑρμᾶς κατὰ τὰς ὁδοὺς ἐν
greek_tokens: qaumazw autos epi sofia epibouleuw au o en o agrew paideuw isthmi autos ermazw kata o odos en mesos o astu kai o dhmos ekastos epeita o sofia o eautou os su manqanw kai os autos eceuriskw eklegw o ageomai sofos eimi outos autos enteinw eis elegeion eautou poihma kai epideigma o sofia epigrafw
english_head: he proceeded next, with the design of educating those of the countryside, to set up figures of Hermes for them along the roads in the midst of the city and every district town; and then, after selecting from his own wise lore, both learnt from others and discovered for himself, the things that he considered the wisest, he threw these into elegiac form and inscribed them on the figures as verses of his own and testimonies of his wisdom, so that in the first place

greek_head: ἵνα πρῶτον μὲν τὰ ἐν Δελφοῖς γράμματα τὰ σοφὰ ταῦτα μὴ θαυμάζοιεν οἱ πολῖται αὐτοῦ τό τε Γνῶθι σαυτόν
greek_tokens: ina proteros men o en delfoi gramma o sofos outos mh qaumazw e poliths autos o te gignwskw sautou kai o mhdeis agan kai allos o toioutos alla o ipparxos rhma mallon sofos hgeomai epeita pareimi anoos kai katos kai anagignwskw kai geuma lambanw autos o sofia foitaw ek o agra kai epi o loipas paideuw eimi de duo epigramma en men o
english_head: his people should not admire those wise Delphic legends of “Know thyself” and “Nothing overmuch”, and the other sayings of the sort, but should rather regard as wise the utterances of Hipparchus; and that in the second place, through passing up and down and reading his words and acquiring a taste for his wisdom, they might resort hither from the country for the completion of their education. There are two such inscriptions of his: on the left side

greek_head: ἐπ' ἀριστερὰ τοῦ Ἑρμοῦ ἑκάστου ἐπιγέγραπται λέγων ὁ Ἑρμῆς ὅτι ἐν μέσῳ τοῦ ἄστεος καὶ τοῦ δήμου ἕστηκεν ἐν
greek_tokens: epi aristeros o ermhs ekastos epigrafw legos o ermazw ostis en mesos o astu kai o dhmos isthmi en de o epi decia mnhma ode ipparxos steixw dikaios fronew fhmi eimi de o poihma kai allos en allos ermhs polus kai kalos epigrafw eimi de dh kai outos epi o steiriakh odaw en os legw
english_head: of each Hermes there is one in which the god says that he stands in the midst of the city or the township, while on the right side he says: The memorial of Hipparchus: walk with just intent. There are many other fine inscriptions from his poems on other figures of Hermes, and this one in particular, on the Steiria road, in which he says:

greek_head: μνῆμα τόδ' Ἱππάρχου μὴ φίλον ἐξαπάτα ἐγὼ οὖν σὲ ἐμοὶ ὄντα φίλον οὐ δήπου τολμῴην ἂν ἐξαπατᾶν
greek_tokens: mnhma ode ipparxos mh filos ecapath egw oun sos egw eimi filos ou dhpou tolmaw an ecapath kai ekeinos toioutos eimi apistew e kai apoqnhskw treis eths turanneuw aqhnaios upo o adelfos autos ippios kai pas an o palaios akouw ostis outos monos o eths turannis gignomai en aqhnh o de allos xronos eggus tis zaw aqhnaios wsper epi kronos basileuw legw de upo o xarieis anqrwpos kai o qanatos autos
english_head: The memorial of Hipparchus: deceive not a friend. I therefore should never dare, I am sure, to deceive you, who are my friend, or disobey the great Hipparchus, after whose death the Athenians were for three years under the despotic rule of his brother Hippias, and you might have heard anyone of the earlier period say that it was only in these years that there was despotism in Athens, and that at all other times the Athenians lived very much as in the reign of Cronos. And the subtler sort of people say

greek_head: γενέσθαι οὐ δι' ἃ οἱ πολλοὶ ᾠήθησαν διὰ τὴν τῆς ἀδελφῆς ἀτιμίαν τῆς κανηφορίας ἐπεὶ τοῦτό γε εὔηθες ἀλλὰ τὸν μὲν
greek_tokens: gignomai ou dia o e polus oiomai dia o o adelfh atimia o kanhforia epei outos ge euhqhs alla o men armodios gignomai paidikon o aristogeitwn kai paideuw upo ekeinos megas de fronew ara kai o aristogeitwn epi o paideuw anqrwpos kai antagwnisths ageomai eimi o ipparxos en ekeinos de o xronos autos o
english_head: that Hipparchus’s death was due, not to the cause supposed by most—the disqualification of the assassin’s sister from bearing the basket, for that is a silly motive—but because Harmodius had become the favorite of Aristogeiton and had been educated by him. Thus Aristogeiton also prided himself on educating people, and he regarded Hipparchus as a dangerous rival. And at that time, it is said, Harmodius

greek_head: Ἁρμόδιον τυγχάνειν ἐρῶντά τινος τῶν νέων τε καὶ καλῶν καὶ γενναίων τῶν τότε καὶ λέγουσι τοὔνομα αὐτοῦ ἐγὼ δὲ
greek_tokens: armodios tugxanw eraw tis o neos te kai kalh kai gennaios o tote kai legw onoma autos egw de ou mimnhskw o oun neaniskos outos teos men qaumazw o te armodios kai o aristogeitwn ws sofos epeita suggignomai o ipparxos katafronew ekeinos kai o perialgew outos o atimia outws apokteinw o ipparxos kinduneuw toinun eimi swkraths h ou filos egw hgeomai h ei hgeomai filos ou peiqw ipparxos egw gar
english_head: happened to be himself in love with one of the handsome and well-born youths of the day; they do tell his name, but I cannot remember it. Well, for a while this youth admired both Harmodius and Aristogeiton as wise men, but afterwards, when he associated with Hipparchus, he despised them, and they were so overcome with the pain of this “disqualification” that they slew Hipparchus. It would seem, then, Socrates, either that you do not regard me as your friend, or if you do, that you do not obey Hipparchus.

greek_head: ὅπως οὐ σὺ ἐμὲ ἐξαπατᾷς οὐκ οἶδ' ὅντινα μέντοι τρόπον ἐν τοῖς λόγοις οὐ δύναμαι πεισθῆναι
greek_tokens: opws ou su egw ecapataw ou oida ostis mentoi tropos en o logos ou dunamai peiqw alla meis kai wsper pesseuw eqelw su en o logos anatiqhmi ostis boulomai o erw ina mh oih ecapataw poteros gar outos su anaqew ws ou o agaqos pas epiqumew anqrwpos mh egw ge alla ws o zhmiow kai o zhmia ou kakos mh egw ge alla ws ou o zhmia kai o zhmiow o kerdos kai o kerdainw enantion
english_head: For that you are not deceiving me—though I cannot tell how you contrive it—in your talk, is more than I can believe. Well now, as though we were playing draughts, I am willing to let you revoke, as you please, anything you have said in carrying on the discussion, in order that you may not think you are being deceived. So tell me, shall I revoke for you the statement that all men desire good things? No, thank you. Well, that suffering loss, or loss, is an evil? No, thank you. Well, that gain, or making gain, is the opposite of loss, or suffering loss?

greek_head: Μηδὲ τοῦτο Ἀλλ' ὡς ἐναντίον ὂν τῷ κακῷ οὐκ ἀγαθόν ἐστι τὸ
greek_tokens: mhde outos alla ws enantion eimi o kakazw ou agaqos eimi o kerdainw outi pas ge outos egw anatiqhmi dokew ara su ws eoika o kerdos o men tis agaqos eimi o de tis kakos egw anatiqhmi toinun sos outos eimi gar dh kerdos tis agaqos kai eteros kerdos tis kakos kerdos de ge oudeis mallon eimi autos o agaqos h o kakos eimi gar pws egw erwtaw egw frazw siteomai eimi tis agaqos te kai kakos
english_head: Nor that either. Well, that making gain, as the opposite of evil, is a good? Nothing of all this do I bid you revoke for me. You think, then, it seems, that some gain is good, and some evil. I do. Well then, I revoke so much for you; so let us assume that some gain is good, and some other gain evil. But the good sort is no more gain than the evil sort, is it? What do you mean by this question? I will explain. Is there both good and evil food?

greek_head: Ναί Ἆρ' οὖν μᾶλλόν τι αὐτῶν ἐστι τὸ ἕτερον τοῦ ἑτέρου
greek_tokens: nai ara oun mallon tis autos eimi o eteros o eteros siteomai h omoios outos ge sition eimi amfoteros kai outos ge oudeis diaferw o eteros o eteros kata o siteomai eimi alla h o men autos agaqos o de kakos nai oukoun kai poton kai allos pas osos o eimi tautos eimi o men pasxw agaqos eimi o de kakos oudeis ekeinos ge diaferw o eteros o eteros h o
english_head: Yes. And is the one sort more food than the other, or are they both similarly this same thing, food, and in this respect does the one differ no wise from the other, in being food, but only in the fact of the one being good and the other evil? Yes. And so with drink and every other class of things that exist, when some things in any class come to be good, and others evil, one thing does not differ from another in that respect whereby they are the same? For instance,

greek_head: αὐτό ἐστιν ὥσπερ ἄνθρωπος δήπου ὁ μὲν χρηστός ἐστιν ὁ δὲ πονηρός
greek_tokens: autos eimi wsper anqrwpos dhpou o men xrhstos eimi o de ponhros nai alla anqrwpos ge oimh oudeteros oudeteros oute mallon oute hsswn eimi oute o xrhstos o ponhros oute o ponhros o xrhstos alhqhs legw oukoun outws kai peri o kerdos dianaw ws kerdos ge omoios eimi kai o ponhros kai o xrhstos anagkh oudeis ara mallon kerdainw o o xrhstos kerdos exw h o ponhros oukoun mallon ge kerdos fainw
english_head: one man, I suppose, is virtuous, and another wicked. Yes. But neither of them, I conceive, is more or less man than the other—neither the virtuous than the wicked, nor the wicked than the virtuous. What you say is true. Then are we to take the same view of gain also, that both the wicked and the virtuous sort are similarly gain? Necessarily. So he who has virtuous gain is no whit the more a gainer than he who has wicked gain: neither sort

greek_head: οὐδέτερον ὄν ὡς ὁμολογοῦμεν Ναί
greek_tokens: oudeteros eimi ws omologew nai oudeteros gar autos oute o mallon oute o hsswn proseimi ou gar dh o dh toioutos pragma pws an tis mallon h hsswn otioun an poiew h pasxw os mhdeteros outos proseimi adunatos epei toinun kerdos men omoios eimi amfoteros kai kerdaleos outos dh deomai egw episkeptomai dia tis pote amfoteros autos kerdos kalew tis tautos en amfoteros oraw
english_head: is found to be more gain, as we agree. Yes. For neither of them has addition of either more or less. No, indeed. And how could one do or suffer anything more or less with a thing of this sort, that had neither of these additions? Impossible. Since, therefore, both of these are gains and gain-making affairs, we must now consider what it can be that leads you to call both of them gain:

greek_head: ὥσπερ ἂν εἰ ἃ σύ με ἠρώτας τὰ νυνδή διὰ τί ποτε καὶ τὸ ἀγαθὸν σιτίον καὶ τὸ κακὸν σιτίον ὁμοίως ἀμφότερα σιτία
greek_tokens: wsper an ei o su egw erwtaw o nundi dia tis pote kai o agaqos siteomai kai o kakos siteomai omoios amfoteros sition kalew eipon an su dioti amfoteros chra trofh swma eimi dia outos egw outos gar eimi siteomai kan su pou egw omologew eimi gar egw kai peri potaomai oun o autos an tropos eaw o apokrisls ostis o o swma ugrazw trefw ean xrhstos
english_head: what is it that you see to be the same in both? Suppose you were to ask me, in those instances that I gave just now, what it is that leads me to call both good food and evil food alike food, I should tell you— for this reason, because both are a dry sustenance of the body. For that, I am sure you would agree, is what food is, would you not? I would. And so too about drink the answer would be on the same lines, that the wet sustenance of the body,

greek_head: ἐάντε πονηρὰ ᾖ τοῦτο τὸ ὄνομά ἐστι ποτόν καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις ὡσαύτως πειρῶ οὖν καὶ σὺ ἐμὲ μιμεῖσθαι οὕτως
greek_tokens: ean ponhros eimi outos o onoma eimi poton kai o allos wsautws peiraw oun kai su egw mimeomai outws apokrinw o xrhstos kerdos kai o ponhros kerdos kerdos fhmi amfoteros eimi tis o autos en autos oraw ostis dh kai outos kerdos eimi ei de au mh autos exis apokrinw alla egw legw skopaw ara kerdos legw pas kthma o an tis ktaomai h mhdeis analiskw h elasswn analiskw pleos lambanw
english_head: whether it be wholesome or pernicious, has this name of drink; and likewise with the rest. Try therefore on your part to imitate my method of answering. When you say that virtuous gain and wicked gain are both gain, what is it that you see to be the same in them, judging it to be the actual element of gain? And if again you are yourself unable to answer, just let me put it for your consideration, whether you describe as gain every acquisition that one has acquired either with no expense, or as a profit over and above one’s expense.

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε δοκῶ τοῦτο καλεῖν κέρδος Ἆρα καὶ τὰ τοιάδε λέγεις ἐάν τις ἑστιαθείς μηδὲν
greek_tokens: egw dokazw outos kalew kerdos ara kai o toiosde legw ean tis estiaw mhdeis analiskw alla euwxew nosos ktaomai ma diw ou egw ugieia de ktaomai apo estiasis kerdos an ktaomai h zhmia kerdos ou ara outos ge eimi kerdos o otioun kthma ktaomai ou mentoi poteros ou ean kakos h oude an agaqos otioun ktaomai ou kerdos ktaomai fainw ean ge agaqos
english_head: I believe that is what I call gain. Do you include a case where, after enjoying a banquet at which one has had much good cheer without any expense, one acquires an illness? Upon my word, not I. And if one acquired health from attending a banquet, would one acquire gain or loss? Gain. Hence gain is not just acquiring any acquisition. No, indeed. Do you mean, not if it is evil? Or will one acquire no gain even if one acquires something good? Apparently one will, if it is good.

greek_head: Ἐὰν δὲ κακόν οὐ ζημίαν κτήσεται Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: ean de kakos ou zhmia ktaomai egw dokew oraw oun ws palin au peritrexw eis o autos o men kerdos agaqos fainw o de zhmia kakos aforaw egw ostis eipon ou adikos ge su aforaw eti gar kai ode apokrinw ean tis elasswn analiskw pleos ktaomai fhmi kerdos eimi outi kakos ge legos alla ean xrusion h argurion elasswn analiskw pleos lambanw
english_head: And if it is evil, will not one acquire loss? I think so. You see, then, how you are running round again to the same old point? Gain is found to be good, and loss evil. For my part, I cannot tell what to say. And not without good reason, sir. Now answer this further question: you say that if one acquires more than the amount one has spent, it is gain? I do not mean, when it is evil, but if one gets more gold or silver than one has spent. Now, I am just going to ask you about that. Tell me,

greek_head: Καὶ ἐγὼ μέλλω τοῦτο ἐρήσεσθαι φέρε γάρ ἐάν τις χρυσίου σταθμὸν ἥμισυν ἀναλώσας διπλάσιον λάβῃ ἀργυρίου
greek_tokens: kai egw mellw outos eromai ferw gar ean tis xruseos staqmon hmisus analiskw diplasios lambanw argurion kerdos h zhmia lambanw zhmia dhpou eimi swkraths anti dwdekastasios gar distasios autos kaqisthmi o xrusion kai meis pleos ge lambanw h ou pleos eimi o diplasios o hmisus outi o acios ge argurion xruseos deomai ara ws eoika o kerdos outos proseimi o acios nun goun o men argurion pleos eimi o xruseos ou fhmi acios eimi o de xrusion elasswn eimi acios fhmi eimi
english_head: if one spends half a pound of gold and gets double that weight in silver, has one got gain or loss? Loss, I presume, Socrates for one’s gold is reduced to twice, instead of twelve times, the value of silver. But you see, one has got more; or is double not more than half? Not in worth, the one being silver and the other gold. So gain, it seems, must have this addition of worth. At least, you now say that silver, though more than gold, is not worth as much, and that gold, though less, is of equal worth.

greek_head: Σφόδρα ἔχει γὰρ οὕτως Τὸ μὲν ἄξιον ἄρα κερδαλέον ἐστίν ἐάντε σμικρὸν
greek_tokens: sfodra exis gar outws o men acios ara kerdaleos eimi ean mikros eimi ean megas o de anacios akerdhs nai o de acios legw acios eimi allos tis h ktaomai nai ktaomai o de acios au legw ktaomai o anwfelhs h o wfelimos o wfelimos dhpou
english_head: Assuredly, for that is the case. Then the valuable is what produces gain, whether it be small or great, and the valueless produces no gain. Yes. And by the valuable you mean simply, valuable to possess? Yes, to possess. And again, by what is valuable to possess, do you mean the unprofitable or the profitable? The profitable, I presume. And the profitable is good? Yes.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν τὸ ὠφέλιμον ἀγαθόν ἐστιν Ναί
greek_tokens: oukoun o wfelimos agaqos eimi nai oukoun eimi andreios pas ou o kerdaleos agaqos au palin tritos h tetartos hkw egw omologew eoika mnhmoneuw oun oqen egw outos o logos gignomai oimh ge ei de mh egw su upomimnhskw amfiamfisbhtew egw o agaqos mh pas o kerdos boulomai kerdainw alla o kerdos agaqos o de ponhros mh naixi
english_head: And so, most valiant of men, have we not here once more, for the third or fourth time, the admission that what produces gain is good? So it seems. Then do you remember the point from which this discussion of ours arose? I think I do. In case you do not, I will remind you. You maintained against me that good men do not wish to make all sorts of gain, but only those gains that are good, and not those that are wicked. Yes.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν νῦν πάντα τὰ κέρδη ὁ λόγος ἡμᾶς ἠνάγκακε καὶ σμικρὰ καὶ μεγάλα ὁμολογεῖν ἀγαθὰ εἶναι
greek_tokens: oukoun nun pas o kerdos o logos egw anagkazw kai mikros kai megas omologew agaqos eimi anagkazw gar eimi swkraths mallon egw ge h peiqw alla isos meta outos kai peiqw an nun de oun eite peiqw eite opws exis sumfhmi goun egw pas o kerdos agaqos eimi kai mikros kai megas omologew gar oun o de xrhstos anqrwpos boulomai agaqos omologew apas apas h ou omologew
english_head: And now the argument has compelled us to acknowledge that all gains, both small and great, are good? Yes, it has compelled me, at least, Socrates, rather than persuaded me. Well, later on, perhaps, it might also persuade you. Now, however, whether you are persuaded or whatever is your feeling, you at least agree with me that all gains are good, both small and great ones. Yes, I do admit it. And you admit that virtuous men all wish for all good things, do you not? I do.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὲν δὴ τούς γε πονηροὺς αὐτὸς εἶπες ὅτι καὶ σμικρὰ καὶ μεγάλα κέρδη φιλοῦσιν
greek_tokens: alla men dh o ge ponhros autos eipon ostis kai mikros kai megas kerdos filew eipon oukoun kata o sos logos pas anqrwpos filokerdew an eien kai e xrhstos kai e ponhros fainw ou ara orqos oneidizw ei tis tis oneidizw filokerdew eimi tugxanw gar kai o outos oneidizw autos toioutos eimi
english_head: But, you know, you stated yourself that wicked men love both small and great gains. I did. And so, by your account, all men will be lovers of gain, whether they be virtuous or wicked. Apparently. Hence it is not right to reproach anybody with being a lover of gain: for he who makes this reproach is actually such an one himself.
